# Understanding Color — Composition and Harmony for Painters

**Source:** [Florent Farges - arts](https://www.youtube.com/@FlorentFarges) (YouTube)
**Date:** 2022-07-15
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=p0rVUhXnmpY
**Duration:** 14:42
**Views:** 314,522

## Overview

A painter's perspective on color harmony with three key concepts: complementary colors via spectral reflectance, the Dominant-Tonic-Accent system, and the 60-30-10 rule. Bridges color science with practical painting composition.

## 1. Complementary Colors (via spectral reflectance)

**Key demonstration:** Shows spectral reflectance graphs of cadmium red (PR108) and cobalt teal blue (PG50) — they cover opposite portions of the visible spectrum. "One is composed of all the nuances of the spectrum that are missing from the other."

**Visual complement test:** Stare at a colored square for 20-30 seconds → remove it → see the afterimage on white background. This retinal persistence reveals the TRUE visual complement.

**Result:** The visual complements match CMY, not RYB:

- Red ↔ Cyan
- Yellow ↔ Blue
- Magenta ↔ Green

> "These pairs do not work in a model where red, blue, and yellow are primary colors."

## 2. Dominant — Tonic — Accent (Farges's system)

A music-inspired terminology for the three roles colors play in a composition:

| Role         | Definition                              | Character                                           |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| **Dominant** | Largest surface area                    | Low chroma, sets the overall tone                   |
| **Tonic**    | The "main color" — the one you remember | Medium-high chroma, defines the painting's identity |
| **Accent**   | Small, vivid touch                      | Highest chroma, draws the eye like sugar            |

### The Chroma Balance Rule

> "Chroma is a little bit like sugar for the eye — the taste is better if it's not excessive."

**Scale analogy:** a very vivid color on one side, a dull color on the other. To balance: the duller color must occupy MORE space. The issue isn't just hue choice — it's chroma × area.

### Key Insight

> "Color harmony rarely comes when colors are all equally vivid next to each other — which creates conflicts. Harmony is more easily achieved if one color keeps a low profile to allow the others to capture attention."

## 3. The 60-30-10 Rule (applied)

| %   | Role     | Character                                 |
| --- | -------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| 60% | Dominant | Neutral/low chroma — sets the environment |
| 30% | Tonic    | Secondary color — the memorable identity  |
| 10% | Accent   | Vivid pop — draws attention to key areas  |

### Historical examples:

- **Vermeer, _The Geographer_:** 60% beige/brown background, 30% deep blue coat, 10% red accent
- **Clausen, _The Stone Pickers_:** 60% light green fields, 30% linen/sky, 10% red fabric

> "I'm not saying Vermeer had this rule in mind — he most likely followed his intuition. But it still led to very harmonious and elegant compositions."

## Connection to Other References

- **Color Nerd's harmony lecture:** confirms character-first > hue-first; chroma+lightness determine mood
- **Aladdin analysis (J. Holt):** same principle — "peacemaker" bridge colors = Farges's dominant; "stowaway" = accent
- **The Futur's 60-30-10 video:** same rule from a design perspective
- **Harvey Rayner's Fontana:** accent types (salt/herbs/spices) map to similar roles

## Practical Advice

- "Neutral colors, cool and warm grays and browns, allow the highlights to really pop"
- "Take advantage of the almost infinite variety of low chroma nuances"
- "Keep the most intense colors for a few well-chosen areas"
- "Nothing is stopping you from breaking the rules and creating imbalance and disharmony"
